title: Euro 2008

infobox.value: [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team") [David Villa](/wiki/David_Villa "David Villa") (4 goals)

or simply **Euro 2008**, was the 13th [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship"), a quadrennial [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") tournament contested by the member nations of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") (the Union of European Football Associations). It took place in Austria and Switzerland (both hosting the tournament for the first time) from 7 to 29 June 2008.

International football competition

The **2008 UEFA European Football Championship**, commonly referred to as **UEFA Euro 2008** or simply **Euro 2008**, was the 13th [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship"), a quadrennial [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") tournament contested by the member nations of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") (the Union of European Football Associations). It took place in Austria and Switzerland (both hosting the tournament for the first time) from 7 to 29 June 2008.

The tournament was won by [Spain](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_football_team "Spain men's national football team"), who defeated [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") 1–0 in the [final](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_Final "UEFA Euro 2008 Final"). Spain were only the second nation to win all their group stage fixtures and then the European Championship itself, matching France's achievement from [1984](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1984_Final "UEFA Euro 1984 Final"). Spain were also the first team since Germany in [1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996") to win the tournament undefeated.

[Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team") were the defending champions going into the tournament, having won [UEFA Euro 2004](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004"). They recorded the worst finish in Euro 2008, losing their three group fixtures and collecting the least prize money. Throughout 31 matches, the participating nations totalled 77 goals, the same as the previous tournament.

[Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team") automatically qualified as hosts; the remaining 14 teams were determined through a [qualifying tournament](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying"), played between August 2006 and November 2007. As European champions, Spain earned the right to compete in the [2009 FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/2009_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2009 FIFA Confederations Cup") in South Africa.

## Bid process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Bid process")]

Main article: [UEFA Euro 2008 bids](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_bids "UEFA Euro 2008 bids")

Austria and Switzerland jointly bid to host the games, and facing competition from six other bids: [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina")–[Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece")–[Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), a 4-way [Nordic](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") bid (from [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden")), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland")–[Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland").[[1]](#cite_note-BBC_contenders-1) Austria and Hungary had previously bid together to host [Euro 2004](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004"), losing out to [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), while Sweden had hosted [Euro 1992](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1992 "UEFA Euro 1992").[[1]](#cite_note-BBC_contenders-1)

Austria–Switzerland, Hungary, Greece–Turkey and the Nordic bid were recommended, in that order, before the final vote by [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA")'s National Teams Committee.[[2]](#cite_note-Mihir-2)

The final vote by the UEFA executive committee was:[[2]](#cite_note-Mihir-2)

1. Austria–Switzerland
2. Hungary
3. Greece–Turkey
4. Nordic
5. Scotland–Ireland
6. Russia
7. Bosnia and Herzegovina–Croatia

The Austria–Switzerland bid became the second successful joint bid in the competition's history, following the [UEFA Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000") hosted by [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"). The [following tournament](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"), held in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), became the third jointly hosted tournament.

## Summary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Summary")]

[Qualification](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying") for Euro 2008 started in August 2006, just over a month after the end of the [2006 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup"). The qualifying tournament was contested by national teams from each of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA")'s member associations except [Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team"), who had automatically qualified for the final tournament as hosts, and [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro_national_football_team "Montenegro national football team"), who came into existence too late to be admitted to UEFA. [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") was the only seeded team not to qualify for the tournament proper, whereas [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_football_team "Russia national football team") was the only unseeded one to qualify. The tournament also marked the debuts of Austria and [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team").

The draw for the final tournament took place on 2 December 2007, and saw [Group C](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_Group_C "UEFA Euro 2008 Group C") immediately labelled as the "[group of death](/wiki/Group_of_death "Group of death")", with [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team"), [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") competing for the two qualifying places. In contrast, [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") and [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team") were deemed to have an easy draw, as the tournament structure meant they could not meet Italy, France, the Netherlands or [Spain](/wiki/Spain_men%27s_national_football_team "Spain men's national football team") until the final.

In the group stage, [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_national_football_team "Croatia national football team"), Spain and the Netherlands all qualified with maximum points. Austria and Switzerland were not expected to progress, despite the advantage of being the hosts. In [Group A](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_Group_A "UEFA Euro 2008 Group A"), the Swiss lost their captain, [Alexander Frei](/wiki/Alexander_Frei "Alexander Frei"), to injury in their first game and became the first team to be eliminated from the tournament, after losing their first two matches. Switzerland managed to beat the group winner Portugal in their last game.

In [Group B](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_Group_B "UEFA Euro 2008 Group B"), Austria managed to set up a decisive final game against Germany, dubbed "Austria's final".[[3]](#cite_note-3) However, they lost by one goal, making Euro 2008 the first European Championship not to have one of the host nations present in the knockout stage. In an exciting final game in Group A, an injury- and suspension-hit [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team") came back from 2–0 down to beat the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_national_football_team "Czech Republic national football team") 3–2, after an uncharacteristic handling mistake by [Petr Čech](/wiki/Petr_%C4%8Cech "Petr Čech"), in the last few minutes, left [Nihat Kahveci](/wiki/Nihat_Kahveci "Nihat Kahveci") with the simplest of finishes.

In the same game, goalkeeper [Volkan Demirel](/wiki/Volkan_Demirel "Volkan Demirel") was shown a red card for pushing Czech striker [Jan Koller](/wiki/Jan_Koller "Jan Koller") to the ground. The Turks joined Portugal as the qualifiers from Group A. France were the high-profile victims of Group C, recording just one point from a goalless draw against Romania in their opening game. Italy beat the French, on the final day, to finish on four points and join the Netherlands in the quarter-finals. Finally, in [Group D](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_Group_D "UEFA Euro 2008 Group D"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team") failed to reproduce the form of their shock [2004 win](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004"), and ended the tournament with no points. Russia qualified at the expense of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team"), after beating them in a final game decider, joining Spain in the knockout stage.

Torrential rain during the Group A match between Switzerland and Turkey on 11 June resulted in the pitch at [St. Jakob-Park](/wiki/St._Jakob-Park "St. Jakob-Park") in [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel") requiring to be re-laid. The new pitch was installed in advance of the quarter-final match between Portugal and Germany on 19 June.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In the quarter-finals, the Portuguese team was unable to give their coach, [Luiz Felipe Scolari](/wiki/Luiz_Felipe_Scolari "Luiz Felipe Scolari"), a fitting send-off – following the mid-tournament announcement that Scolari would be leaving to join English club [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") – losing in an exciting game against Germany. Turkey continued their streak of last-gasp wins, equalising at the end of extra-time against Croatia and advancing on penalties. Coached by Dutchman [Guus Hiddink](/wiki/Guus_Hiddink "Guus Hiddink"), Russia eliminated the Netherlands with two extra-time goals. The last quarter-final match saw Spain defeat Italy on penalties, after a goalless draw in regular time.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Celebracion_Eurocopa_1.jpg/330px-Celebracion_Eurocopa_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Celebracion_Eurocopa_1.jpg)

The Spanish football team touring Madrid as champions

Turkey's progress was halted by Germany in the semi-finals. Turkey entered the game with nine of their squad members missing due to injury or suspension, but still scored the first goal. Later, they levelled the score at 2–2, before Germany scored the winning goal in the final minute. The world television feed of the match was intermittently lost during the match, which prevented the broadcast of Germany's second goal.

This was due to a thunderstorm at the broadcasting relay station in Austria, despite the game being played in Switzerland. Swiss Television [SRG SSR](/wiki/SRG_SSR "SRG SSR") still had a feed, because of their own broadcasting facilities at the venue. During the lost world feed, German and Austrian television [ZDF](/wiki/ZDF "ZDF") and [ORF](/wiki/ORF_(broadcaster) "ORF (broadcaster)") started to broadcast the feed of German-speaking Swiss channel [SF 1](/wiki/SRF_1 "SRF 1").

This act ensured that the German goal was actually broadcast in Germany although not in Turkey.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Spain won the second semi-final against Russia by three goals to nil, through second-half goals from [Xavi](/wiki/Xavi_(footballer,_born_1980) "Xavi (footballer, born 1980)"), [Daniel Güiza](/wiki/Daniel_G%C3%BCiza "Daniel Güiza") and [David Silva](/wiki/David_Silva "David Silva"), earning Spain their first appearance in a major final for 24 years.

In the final, held at [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna")'s [Ernst-Happel-Stadion](/wiki/Ernst-Happel-Stadion "Ernst-Happel-Stadion"), Spain became European champions for the second time after [Fernando Torres](/wiki/Fernando_Torres "Fernando Torres")' first-half goal proved enough to defeat Germany. Though Germany had a strong start, Spain started to look more dangerous after they had settled.

After half an hour, Xavi played a pass in behind the Germany back line towards Torres, who outmuscled a hesitant [Philipp Lahm](/wiki/Philipp_Lahm "Philipp Lahm") and clipped the ball over the diving [Jens Lehmann](/wiki/Jens_Lehmann "Jens Lehmann") and just inside the far post. That goal proved to be the only goal of the game, which Spain dominated despite Germany having the majority of the possession,[[6]](#cite_note-6) and Spain were crowned UEFA Euro 2008 champions.

## Qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Qualification")]

Main article: [UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying")

The draw for the qualifying round took place in [Montreux](/wiki/Montreux "Montreux"), Switzerland on 27 January 2006 at 12:00 [CET](/wiki/Central_European_Time "Central European Time").

The qualifying process commenced a month after the [2006 World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup"). Austria and Switzerland automatically qualified for the tournament finals as host nations.

The qualifying format was changed compared to previous tournaments. The winners and runners-up from seven groups automatically qualified for the Championship, with the hosts filling the other two slots in the 16-team tournament. The change means there were no [play-offs](/wiki/Play-off "Play-off") between teams finishing in second place in the groups – they qualified directly for the finals. Teams that finished outside the top two positions in their groups failed to qualify. Group A contained eight teams, and the others contained seven.

12 out of 16 teams who qualified for previous tournament also qualified, but the hosts, Austria, and Poland made their debuts at the European Championship. Romania and Turkey returned after missing out the 2004 tournament. For the first time since 1984, all five teams from the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") failed to qualify, including 2006 World Cup quarter-finalists England.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Other notable absentees were 2006 World Cup quarter-finalists [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine_national_football_team "Ukraine national football team"), and [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_national_football_team "Denmark national football team"), who failed to qualify for the first time since 1980. [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_national_football_team "Bulgaria national football team") and [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia_national_football_team "Latvia national football team") also failed to qualify after playing in Euro 2004.

As of 2024, this is the last time England and Ukraine have failed to qualify for the European Championship.

### Final draw

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Final draw")]

The draw for the final tournament took place on 2 December 2007 at the [Lucerne Culture and Congress Centre](/wiki/Lucerne_Culture_and_Congress_Centre "Lucerne Culture and Congress Centre") in Switzerland.[[8]](#cite_note-draw-12)

As was the case at the 2000 and 2004 finals, the finalists were divided into four [seeding](/wiki/Single-elimination_tournament#Seeding "Single-elimination tournament") pots, based on the 2007 edition of the [UEFA national team coefficient](/wiki/UEFA_coefficient#Men's_national_team_coefficient "UEFA coefficient") ranking, which measured performance of teams in the [2006 FIFA World Cup qualifying](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2006 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)") and [Euro 2008 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying"),[[9]](#cite_note-2007ranking-13) with each group having one team drawn from each pot. In a return to the format used at [Euro 1992](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1992 "UEFA Euro 1992") and [Euro 1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996") the games in each group were held at just two stadia, with the seeded team playing all three matches in the same city. [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team") and [Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team"), as co-hosts, were automatically assigned to positions A1 and B1, respectively. The remaining 14 teams were split into four pots, with title-holders [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team") seeded alongside the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") in Pot 1.[[10]](#cite_note-14)

UEFA came under heavy criticism from [Raymond Domenech](/wiki/Raymond_Domenech "Raymond Domenech"), manager of [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"), who was not satisfied with his team's position in the draw,[[11]](#cite_note-15) and was also in favour of having [2006 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup") winners [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") as top seed.[[12]](#cite_note-16) On 22 November 2007, Giorgio Marchetti, UEFA's professional football director, announced that a review of the coefficient ranking system was under way for future [European Championships](/wiki/UEFA_European_Football_Championship "UEFA European Football Championship").[[8]](#cite_note-draw-12)

Next step was to draw all teams in the order from Pot 4, Pot 3 and Pot 2; and for these teams the next group positions 2/3/4 were drawn separately from an extra glass bowl, for the purposes of determining the match schedules in each group. Coincidentally, all teams from Pots 2, 3, and 4 drew the exact same group position number as their pot number.[[13]](#cite_note-Draw(part1)-19)[[14]](#cite_note-Draw(part2)-20)

## Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Venues")]

The tournament was played at eight venues throughout the two host nations; four in Austria and four in Switzerland. Each venue had a capacity of at least 30,000 for the tournament; the largest stadium was [Ernst-Happel-Stadion](/wiki/Ernst-Happel-Stadion "Ernst-Happel-Stadion") in Vienna with a capacity of 53,295.[[16]](#cite_note-22) It was for this reason that Ernst-Happel-Stadion hosted the final. Switzerland played all three group stage matches at [St. Jakob Park](/wiki/St._Jakob_Park "St. Jakob Park") in Basel, which also hosted the opening match of the tournament as a compromise for the final being held in Vienna. Austria played all of their group stage matches at Ernst-Happel-Stadion.

In 2004, the [Zürich](/wiki/Z%C3%BCrich "Zürich") venue became a problem for the organisers. Originally, the [Hardturm](/wiki/Hardturm "Hardturm") stadium was to be renovated and used as the city's venue, but legal challenges delayed the plan to a point that would not have allowed the ground to be used in 2008. This created a problem, as the agreement between [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") and the organisers stipulated that four venues would be used in each country. The problem was solved when the organisers proposed renovating [Letzigrund](/wiki/Letzigrund "Letzigrund") instead;[[17]](#cite_note-23) UEFA approved the revised plan in January 2005. The Letzigrund stadium hosted its first football match on 23 September 2007.

### Team base camps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Team base camps")]

Each team had access to a "team base camp" for its stay between the matches.[[18]](#cite_note-basecamps-24) The teams trained and resided in these locations during the tournament, and travelled to games that took place away from their bases.[[19]](#cite_note-25)[[20]](#cite_note-26) The 16 teams validated their option with UEFA on 18 December 2007.[[18]](#cite_note-basecamps-24)

## Squads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Squads")]

Main article: [UEFA Euro 2008 squads](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_squads "UEFA Euro 2008 squads")

Teams were required to select a squad of 23 players, three of whom had to be goalkeepers, with the final squad to be submitted to UEFA by 28 May 2008. If a member of the final squad suffered an injury prior to his team's first game that would keep him out of the entire tournament, another player could be called up to replace him.[[21]](#cite_note-regulations-27)

## Match officials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Match officials")]

On 19 December 2007, UEFA announced twelve referees and twenty-four assistants were selected for the tournament.[[22]](#cite_note-28) In April 2008, after failing a physical fitness test, Norwegian assistant referee Erik Ræstad was replaced by fellow countryman Jan Petter Randen.[[23]](#cite_note-29) Italian referee [Roberto Rosetti](/wiki/Roberto_Rosetti "Roberto Rosetti") was selected to officiate both the opening match between Switzerland and the Czech Republic and the final between Germany and Spain.

## Group stage

Performance of the participating countries during Euro 2008

The teams finishing in the top two positions in each of the four groups progressed to the quarter-finals, while the bottom two teams were eliminated from the tournament.

*All times are local, [CEST](/wiki/Central_European_Summer_Time "Central European Summer Time") ([UTC+2](/wiki/UTC%2B02:00 "UTC+02:00")).*

### Tiebreakers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Tiebreakers")]

For the three-game group stage of this tournament, where two or more teams in a group [tied on an equal number of points](/wiki/Tiebreaker "Tiebreaker"), the finishing positions were determined by the following tie-breaking criteria in the following order:[[21]](#cite_note-regulations-27)

1. number of points obtained in the matches among the teams in question
2. goal difference in the matches among the teams in question (if more than two teams finish equal on points)
3. number of goals scored in the matches among the teams in question (if more than two teams finish equal on points)
4. goal difference in all the group matches
5. number of goals scored in all the group matches
6. coefficient from the qualifying competitions for the 2006 FIFA World Cup and 2006/08 UEFA European Football Championship (points obtained divided by the number of matches played)
7. fair play conduct of the teams (final tournament)
8. drawing of lots

However, these normal criteria would not apply if two teams tied on points, goal difference, goals scored, and goals conceded, played against each other in their final group match, drew that game, and no other team in the group finishes with the same number of points; in that case, the tie would be broken by a [penalty shootout](/wiki/Penalty_shootout_(association_football) "Penalty shootout (association football)").[[21]](#cite_note-regulations-27)

## Knockout stage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Knockout stage")]

Main article: [UEFA Euro 2008 knockout stage](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_knockout_stage "UEFA Euro 2008 knockout stage")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Spain_Euro_08_celebration_2.jpg/250px-Spain_Euro_08_celebration_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spain_Euro_08_celebration_2.jpg)

[Cesc Fàbregas](/wiki/Cesc_F%C3%A0bregas "Cesc Fàbregas") celebrating Spain's Euro 2008 title

The knockout stage was different from that of past tournaments. Teams in groups A and B were separated from teams in groups C and D until the final. This meant that two teams who meet in the same group would meet again in the semi-finals instead of the final if they got this far. Also, in another major change, for the first time in a European Championship, only two venues ([St. Jakob-Park](/wiki/St._Jakob-Park "St. Jakob-Park"), [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel") and [Ernst-Happel-Stadion](/wiki/Ernst-Happel-Stadion "Ernst-Happel-Stadion"), Vienna—the two largest of the eight stadiums used) were used for the seven matches in the knockout stage of the tournament.[[48]](#cite_note-57)

As with every tournament since [UEFA Euro 1984](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1984 "UEFA Euro 1984"), there was no [third place play-off](/wiki/Third_place_play-off "Third place play-off").

*All times are local, [CEST](/wiki/Central_European_Summer_Time "Central European Summer Time") ([UTC+2](/wiki/UTC%2B02:00 "UTC+02:00")).*

Nine players from the winning Spanish team were named in the team of the tournament, while no players knocked out in the group stage were included. ### Prize money

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Prize money")]

[UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") announced that total of €184 million has been offered to the 16 teams competing in this tournament, increasing from €129 million in the [previous tournament](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004"). The distributions as below:[[58]](#cite_note-67)

* Prize for participating: €7.5 million

Extra payment based on teams performances:

* Winner: €7.5 million
* Runner-up: €4.5 million
* Semi-finals: €3 million
* Quarter-finals: €2 million
* Group stage (per match):
  + Win: €1 million
  + Draw: €500,000

Spain, as winners of the tournament and winners of all three of their group stage matches, received a total prize of €23 million, the maximum possible prize money. Greece on the other hand, being the only team to lose all three of their group matches, were the only team to receive nothing more than the €7.5 million participation prize.

### Discipline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Discipline")]

At UEFA Euro 2008, players were suspended from playing in subsequent matches upon the collection of a certain number of yellow or red cards. If a player was shown a red card – whether as a result of two bookable offences or a straight red – that player got suspended from playing in his team's next match. If his team was eliminated from the competition before the end of his suspension, the games carried over to the [2010 FIFA World Cup qualification matches](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2010 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"). A player was also suspended for one match for picking up two yellow cards in separate matches. However, any yellow cards accumulated were annulled once a team got eliminated from the tournament or reached the semi-finals.[[59]](#cite_note-68)

In extreme cases of ill-discipline, UEFA could choose to have a disciplinary panel examine the incident in order to determine whether or not further suspension is required. One case of this at Euro 2008 was the suspension of [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team") goalkeeper [Volkan Demirel](/wiki/Volkan_Demirel "Volkan Demirel") for two matches for pushing [Czech](/wiki/Czech_Republic_national_football_team "Czech Republic national football team") striker [Jan Koller](/wiki/Jan_Koller "Jan Koller").[[60]](#cite_note-69)

## Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Marketing")]

### Television coverage failure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Television coverage failure")]

Three times in the second half of the [semi-final between Germany and Turkey](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_knockout_stage#Germany_vs_Turkey "UEFA Euro 2008 knockout stage"), nearly the entire global television coverage of the game was interrupted. A thunderstorm over Vienna caused technical difficulties in the [International Broadcast Centre](/wiki/International_Broadcast_Centre "International Broadcast Centre") (IBC), which relayed the television feed from the match in Basel, Switzerland, resulting in one or more goals being missed by various audiences.[[61]](#cite_note-70) Various national broadcasters took emergency contingency measures such as reverting to radio broadcasting (for example, the [BBC](/wiki/British_Broadcasting_Corporation "British Broadcasting Corporation") used coverage from [BBC Radio 5 Live](/wiki/BBC_Radio_5_Live "BBC Radio 5 Live"),[[62]](#cite_note-71) Ireland circumvented the problem by having [RTÉ Two](/wiki/RT%C3%89_Two "RTÉ Two")'s studio presenter [Bill O'Herlihy](/wiki/Bill_O%27Herlihy "Bill O'Herlihy") and panellists [Eamon Dunphy](/wiki/Eamon_Dunphy "Eamon Dunphy"), [Johnny Giles](/wiki/Johnny_Giles "Johnny Giles") and [Liam Brady](/wiki/Liam_Brady "Liam Brady") provide emergency discussion on what had happened in the match,[[63]](#cite_note-72) and [Øyvind Alsaker](/wiki/%C3%98yvind_Alsaker "Øyvind Alsaker"), commentator from [Norwegian TV2](/wiki/TV_2_(Norway) "TV 2 (Norway)") picked up his mobile and filmed it over a [3G](/wiki/3G "3G") connection). Only the Swiss public broadcaster [SRG](/wiki/SRG_SSR_id%C3%A9e_suisse "SRG SSR idée suisse") maintained full coverage since it used a direct signal other than the IBC's.[[64]](#cite_note-73)

### New trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: New trophy")]

A new trophy was awarded to the winners of the Euro 2008 tournament. The new version of the [Henri Delaunay](/wiki/Henri_Delaunay "Henri Delaunay") Trophy, created by [Asprey](/wiki/Asprey "Asprey") London,[[65]](#cite_note-74) is almost an exact replica of the original designed by [Arthus-Bertrand](/wiki/Arthus-Bertrand "Arthus-Bertrand"). A small figure juggling a ball on the back of the original has been removed, as has the marble plinth. The silver base of the trophy also had to be enlarged to make it stable. The names of the winning countries that had appeared on the plinth have now been engraved on the back of the trophy, which is made of [sterling silver](/wiki/Sterling_silver "Sterling silver"), weighs 8 kilograms (17.6 lb) and is 60 centimetres (24 in) tall.

### Match ball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Match ball")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Euro_Cup_2008_Final_Vienna.JPG/250px-Euro_Cup_2008_Final_Vienna.JPG)](/wiki/File:Euro_Cup_2008_Final_Vienna.JPG)

A large model of the *adidas Europass* prior to the final between Germany and Spain

The [match ball](/wiki/Association_football_ball "Association football ball") for the finals was unveiled at the draw ceremony. Produced by [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") and named the *[Europass](/wiki/Adidas_Europass "Adidas Europass")*, it is a 14-panel ball in the same construction as the [Teamgeist](/wiki/Adidas_Teamgeist "Adidas Teamgeist"), but with a modified surface design.[[66]](#cite_note-75) A version named the *Europass Gloria* was used in the final.[[67]](#cite_note-76)

There were concerns raised about the match ball, which was claimed to deviate unpredictably in flight, making it difficult to judge for goalkeepers. Notable players to criticise were Germany's [Jens Lehmann](/wiki/Jens_Lehmann "Jens Lehmann") and the Czech Republic's [Petr Čech](/wiki/Petr_%C4%8Cech "Petr Čech").[[68]](#cite_note-77) These claims were disputed by the ball's designer, [Oliver Kahn](/wiki/Oliver_Kahn "Oliver Kahn").

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Music")]

The official melody was composed by [Rollo Armstrong](/wiki/Rollo_Armstrong "Rollo Armstrong") of [Faithless](/wiki/Faithless "Faithless") on behalf of UEFA.[[69]](#cite_note-music-78) The official Euro 2008 song was "[Can You Hear Me](/wiki/Can_You_Hear_Me%3F_(Enrique_Iglesias_song) "Can You Hear Me? (Enrique Iglesias song)")" by [Enrique Iglesias](/wiki/Enrique_Iglesias "Enrique Iglesias"), which was performed live during the official closing ceremony prior to the final in [Ernst Happel Stadion](/wiki/Ernst_Happel_Stadion "Ernst Happel Stadion") in Vienna on 29 June.[[70]](#cite_note-official_song-79)

Two soundtracks, "Like a Superstar" and "Feel the Rush," were recorded by Jamaican [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") artist [Shaggy](/wiki/Shaggy_(musician) "Shaggy (musician)") as mascot songs for Euro 2008. They formed a musical background to video clips featuring the twin mascots Trix and Flix.[[69]](#cite_note-music-78)

The official Swiss song for the tournament was a new version of "Bring en hei" (*Bring him Home*) by Baschi.[[70]](#cite_note-official_song-79) [Christina Stürmer](/wiki/Christina_St%C3%BCrmer "Christina Stürmer") sang the official tournament song of Austrian [ÖFB](/wiki/%C3%96sterreichischer_Fu%C3%9Fballbund "Österreichischer Fußballbund"), "Fieber" (*Fever*).[[71]](#cite_note-80) [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_national_football_team "Croatia national football team") manager [Slaven Bilić](/wiki/Slaven_Bili%C4%87 "Slaven Bilić") recorded his country's official Euro 2008 song, "Vatreno ludilo" ("Fiery Madness"), with his rock group, [Rawbau](/wiki/Rawbau "Rawbau").

"[Seven Nation Army](/wiki/Seven_Nation_Army "Seven Nation Army")" by [The White Stripes](/wiki/The_White_Stripes "The White Stripes") was played when players walked out before kick-off,[[72]](#cite_note-81) and a remix of "Samba de Janeiro" by German dance group [Bellini](/wiki/Paffendorf "Paffendorf") was played after each goal scored in the competition.[[73]](#cite_note-82)[[74]](#cite_note-83)

### Mascots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Mascots")]

See also: [UEFA European Championship mascot](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship_mascot "UEFA European Championship mascot")

The two official mascots for UEFA Euro 2008, were named after a vote from the public of the two host nations from the following options:

* Zigi and Zagi
* Flitz and Bitz
* Trix and Flix

In April 2007, after receiving 36.3% of the vote, Trix and Flix were chosen. "I am sure the mascots and their names will become a vital part of the understanding of the whole event," said Christian Mutschler, the tournament director for Switzerland.[[75]](#cite_note-84) The mascots were unveiled on 27 September 2006, in [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), Austria. Their official début was on 11 October 2006, at the [Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team") vs. [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team") friendly, which ended 2–1.[[76]](#cite_note-85)

### Slogan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=UEFA_Euro_2008&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Slogan")]

The slogan for UEFA Euro 2008 was chosen on 24 January 2007: *Expect Emotions*.[[77]](#cite_note-86) UEFA President [Michel Platini](/wiki/Michel_Platini "Michel Platini") stated, "It describes in a nutshell what the UEFA Euro 2008 has to offer: all kinds of emotions – joy, disappointment, relief or high tension – right up to the final whistle."[[78]](#cite_note-87)
